title: Notapusy

jokes.text: Michael was heading to work when he saw something he had never seen before lunch, his twin sister Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Narrator sets up a dramatic 'something he had never seen before'
jokes.punchline: It's just Lindsay — awake before noon, implying she's habitually lazy
jokes.explanation: The narrator's faux-dramatic build ('something he had never seen before') lands perfectly with a mundane deflation. The joke is deeply character-specific — it only works because Lindsay's laziness is established canon. Economy is excellent: the punchline is embedded in the sentence structure itself.

jokes.text: Lindsay's plan: take the nanny job so Bob Loblaw gives her a free divorce, then marry her so she never has to work again.
jokes.setup: Lindsay claims she 'works' for the attorney now
jokes.punchline: Her entire strategy is a multi-step scheme to never work — delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: Classic Lindsay — an elaborate plan entirely oriented around avoiding any effort. The irony of working so hard at a scheme to never work is perfectly in character and delivered with complete self-seriousness.

jokes.text: Lindsay mentions she got her horse to canter, then says 'I've always wanted to make love on a horse.'
jokes.setup: Innocent equestrian update about the horse's progress
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivots to a sexual fantasy about horses with zero self-awareness
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's total obliviousness to how she sounds is the joke. The speed of the pivot from innocent riding lesson to sexual fantasy is the comedic engine. Slightly crude but executed with Lindsay's signature lack of self-awareness.

jokes.text: Lindsay responds to news of working without a lunge-line with 'That's so funny, because I can put my leg behind my head.'
jokes.setup: Hope Loblaw shares an innocent riding milestone (working without a lunge-line)
jokes.punchline: Lindsay non-sequiturs into a flexible-body boast, revealing she's been mentally auditioning herself as a sexual prospect throughout a child's riding lesson
jokes.explanation: The non-sequitur is perfectly timed — the audience connects before Lindsay explains, giving that satisfying half-second of 'wait...' The joke reveals character (Lindsay is filtering everything through seduction) while staying technically innocent.

jokes.text: Michael asks 'Snog? Is that another one of your new British-isms?' after unconsciously using the word himself
jokes.setup: Michael is falling for Rita and picking up Britishisms unconsciously
jokes.punchline: He uses 'snog' himself and then questions Lindsay for using it — self-unaware irony immediately lampshaded by the narrator
jokes.explanation: The joke works on ironic self-unawareness — Michael critiques Lindsay for the very word he just used. The narrator's dry 'It was' clinches it. Shows Michael's infatuation subtly and comedically.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael had recently fallen bum-over-noggin for a beautiful Englishwoman called Rita.'
jokes.setup: Standard narrator transition explaining Michael's romantic situation
jokes.punchline: 'Bum-over-noggin' is the British equivalent of 'head-over-heels' — a Briticism the narrator adopts, mirroring Michael's own infection by Rita's influence
jokes.explanation: Dense little joke: the narrator himself adopting Britishisms reinforces the theme without a character needing to notice it. Rewards attentive viewers. 'Bum-over-noggin' is genuinely funny as a phrase and the meta-layer of the narrator being 'infected' is elegant.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Did I say snog again? Oh, bloody hell.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been unconsciously adopting British slang from Rita
jokes.punchline: He catches himself using 'bloody hell' — another Briticism — while trying to apologize for using Britishisms
jokes.explanation: Beautifully self-eating joke: the cure IS the disease. Apologizing for a Briticism with another Briticism in the same breath is a perfect escalation. The economy is exceptional — three words ('Oh, bloody hell') deliver the entire second punchline. One of the tighter jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Michael's advice to Lindsay: 'Find something you're good at and share it with her. And while you're at it, share it with us, too.'
jokes.setup: Michael gives advice on winning over Bob Loblaw's daughter
jokes.punchline: The addition of 'share it with us, too' is a blunt acknowledgment that Lindsay has no known talents — delivered without cruelty but with total clarity
jokes.explanation: Sibling cruelty disguised as sincere advice. Michael can't help the dig and doesn't even notice he's made it. Works because it's true and delivered with Michael's signature earnestness.

jokes.text: Michael's phone check: 'Oh my God! Did I miss a call?' — it's a voicemail from Rita saying she's on her way to the train station
jokes.setup: Michael's advice to Lindsay included 'try not to act so desperate'
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately panics about a missed call and sprints to the train station — the desperate act he just cautioned against
jokes.explanation: Perfect hypocrisy joke — Michael violates his own advice in the same breath he gives it. The structure is airtight: advice → immediate contradiction → narrator confirms the obvious. Rewards viewers paying attention to the preceding dialogue.

jokes.text: Hi. Where are you going and how long you going for?
jokes.setup: Michael has raced to the train station, breathless, claiming he wasn't hurrying to stop Rita from leaving
jokes.punchline: His first question is exactly the kind of clingy interrogation that reveals he absolutely WAS hurrying to stop her
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Michael's claim of non-clinginess is instantly demolished by his own first words. The irony is clean and character-specific — Michael always believes his own rationalizations.

jokes.text: Michael's out-of-breath excuse: 'I wasn't hurrying to come stop you. I was out for my daily, uh...' — and then Rita reveals she won an Olympic silver medal in running
jokes.setup: Michael is desperately trying to pretend he wasn't running to the station; he implies he runs regularly
jokes.punchline: Rita is an Olympic silver medalist in running — the one sport he's now falsely claimed to also do, in front of an expert
jokes.explanation: Classic escalating embarrassment. Michael's lie walks directly into the one person who could most expose it. The setup is economical and the punchline lands hard because it comes from Rita, not a narrator note. Good comedic construction.

jokes.text: 'But you take my breath away.' — delivered by a winded, clearly-out-of-shape Michael to an Olympic runner
jokes.setup: Michael is visibly out of breath after running to the station; he's now embarrassed
jokes.punchline: The double meaning of 'you take my breath away' — literally true (he's gasping) and romantic — delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A well-worn phrase redeemed by perfect situational context. The wordplay works because it's simultaneously literally accurate AND romantically deployed. Four words, total economy, lands well.

jokes.text: Narrator explanation + WWII cutaway: 'Rita meant it in the British sense, meaning sweet or gentle, as in pussycat' — cut to soldiers calling a pacifist soldier a 'pussy' as a compliment
jokes.setup: Narrator explains the British meaning of the censored word
jokes.punchline: The WWII cutaway illustrates the British usage with 'The Jerries aren't so bad, they're just led by a rotten apple' — a delightfully anachronistic and historically absurd scene used to explain a wordplay joke
jokes.explanation: The WWII flashback is a wildly unexpected but perfectly logical way to illustrate the etymology of the joke. 'The Jerries aren't so bad, they're just led by a rotten apple' is its own delicious aside — hilariously understated characterization of World War II. The investment in the bit (an entire period-costume cutaway) is proportional to how much the episode is built around this joke.

jokes.text: 'When I miss your lips, I'll put a (bleep) in my mouth and think of you.' Narrator: 'That also had a different meaning over there.'
jokes.setup: The WWII flashback establishing the British meaning of (bleep)
jokes.punchline: A second, more lurid double-entendre is presented deadpan and explained away with eight words
jokes.explanation: Economy of 9.5 — eight words ('That also had a different meaning over there') do all the heavy lifting. The joke piggybacks on the previous structure and escalates it into genuinely shocking territory while remaining technically defensible. The narrator's breezy dismissal is perfect. One of the episode's strongest moments.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'In fact, the only thing George Michael really liked was hanging motionless from the monkey bars. Which the President's Council on Fitness ranks as slightly easier than the slide.'
jokes.setup: Michael claims he and George Michael have never been a good athletic team
jokes.punchline: George Michael's preferred athletic activity is hanging still — the most passive possible engagement with athletic equipment — and the Council on Fitness has specifically ranked it
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'hanging motionless' (not swinging, not playing — motionless) is inspired. Then the show invents an authoritative institutional ranking for this non-activity. The detail that it's 'slightly easier than the slide' — not 'easier than all other activities' but specifically the SLIDE — is an absurdist precision that elevates this significantly. Strong candidate for the episode's best narrator joke.

jokes.text: George Michael wants to go fabric shopping with Ann; Michael says 'I think we can go butcher.'
jokes.setup: George Michael says he's going fabric shopping
jokes.punchline: 'Butcher' as in 'butcher fabric' (British rhyming slang for 'look') — or simply meaning 'more masculine.' The double meaning lands differently depending on viewer knowledge.
jokes.explanation: An intentional or unintentional Britishism (rhyming slang: butcher's hook = look), or simply Michael using 'butcher' as a masculinity synonym. Either way the wordplay is gentle. Serviceable.

jokes.text: Michael's method for remembering Ann's name: 'her body's kind of shaped like a...' — implying 'Annabelle' because Ann's body is bell-shaped
jokes.setup: Michael explains he remembers Ann as 'Annabelle'
jokes.punchline: He trails off, revealing he remembers her name by the shape of her body — implying she's bell-shaped (unflattering)
jokes.explanation: Part of the running 'Ann' gag and one of its best iterations. The trailing off into implication is funnier than saying it outright — Michael doesn't even finish the insult because George Michael (and the audience) fills it in. The running 'her?' joke about Ann is this show at its economical best.

jokes.text: Michael: 'She's the belle of the ball.' — then George Michael reveals Ann is a contestant in the beauty pageant, not running the lights.
jokes.setup: Michael assumes Ann is working the lights at the pageant (not competing in it)
jokes.punchline: George Michael reveals Ann is actually a contestant; Michael's 'she's the belle of the ball' callback resonates as (unintentional) mockery
jokes.explanation: The 'belle of the ball' line works on two levels: it's a sarcastic riff on the bell-shaped body joke AND it transitions into the real reveal. The assumption that Ann would be running the lights rather than competing is a running dismissal of Ann's attractiveness that the show executes consistently.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'It's an inner beauty pageant.' Michael: 'Ah! There it is.'
jokes.setup: Michael is confused why Ann would enter a beauty pageant
jokes.punchline: 'There it is' — Michael immediately understands everything; it's an inner beauty pageant (a euphemism for 'regular people')
jokes.explanation: Possibly the most economical joke in the episode. Two words ('There it is') encapsulate Michael's entire condescending assessment of Ann. The beat — the pause, then the click of understanding — is perfectly structured. The joke doesn't need to say anything more.

jokes.text: The State Fair is going to be a 'Church and State Fair' — the state ran out of money and a church agreed to fund it if they got a presence
jokes.setup: The fair needs funding
jokes.punchline: The solution is a literal violation of church-state separation, played as a mundane logistical compromise
jokes.explanation: The joke is the name itself — 'Church and State Fair' as a pun that also captures a real political concept (church-state separation) treated as an afterthought. Clever conceptual comedy. Enables many subsequent jokes throughout the episode.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'There's no such thing as an inner beauty pageant. They're just gonna pick the chick with the best T and A.'
jokes.setup: George Michael explains the inner beauty pageant
jokes.punchline: Maeby cuts through the euphemism with unfiltered realism; the flashback to her own childhood pageants confirms her thesis
jokes.explanation: Maeby as the cynical truth-teller is perfectly in character. The observation is broadly true and the bluntness is funny. Not exceptional but effective character-comedy.

jokes.text: Narrator: childhood progressive pageants claimed to celebrate beauty in all forms, 'but the winners still tended to have it going on in the conventional sense.'
jokes.setup: Maeby's claim that inner beauty pageants are a sham
jokes.punchline: The narrator's restrained 'still tended to have it going on in the conventional sense' is euphemistic confirmation — the most polite possible way to say Maeby was right
jokes.explanation: The narrator's genteel way of confirming crude truth is one of the show's reliable comic tools. 'Have it going on in the conventional sense' is an elegant, overly polite euphemism for what Maeby said directly.

jokes.text: Lindsay doesn't know where Maeby's hair came from. Maeby: 'Shameless Plugs, on Placentia. They're not licensed, which means big savings.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay comments on Maeby's suspicious hair, implying it might be fake or unusual
jokes.punchline: Maeby casually reveals she's had her hair done at an unlicensed salon called 'Shameless Plugs' on 'Placentia' — both a hair pun and a location gag
jokes.explanation: 'Shameless Plugs' as a hair salon name is a visual pun (hair plugs) plus a meta-joke about the concept of plugging products. 'Placentia' as the street name is a deliberately ugly word used deadpan for comic effect. The 'not licensed, big savings' is Maeby cheerfully accepting risk — very in character.

jokes.text: I'm guessing 'have sex', but that's meeting you more than halfway.
jokes.setup: Lindsay says 'crown me, if you know what I mean'
jokes.punchline: Tobias correctly decodes the euphemism but then notes he had to work hard to get there — implying Lindsay's innuendo wasn't even clear enough to be a decent euphemism
jokes.explanation: Tobias at his finest — his response is both a critique of Lindsay's terrible innuendo AND a Tobias-specific oblivious double entendre (meeting her 'more than halfway' sexually is its own ironic note given their marriage). Perfect economy. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'If I can get Hope this crown, maybe her father might just want to crown me, if you know what I mean.' Tobias: 'I'm guessing have sex, but that's meeting you more than halfway.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay uses a euphemism ('crown me') to hint at her plan to seduce Bob Loblaw
jokes.punchline: Tobias cuts to the chase but notes that he's doing most of the interpretive work — implying the euphemism was so poor that understanding it required more than half the effort
jokes.explanation: Tobias as the oblivious-yet-occasionally-astute character lands this perfectly. 'Meeting you more than halfway' is a genuinely clever reversal of the usual idiom — normally you 'meet someone halfway,' but here Tobias is claiming he's doing over 50% of the communicative labor because Lindsay's euphemism was so bad. Strong line, well-delivered.

jokes.text: Tobias asks someone to clean up the mirror, then the vase — implying he's broken things in a dramatic moment, visible to the audience
jokes.setup: Tobias is delivering his speech about winning back Lindsay's respect
jokes.punchline: Mid-monologue, he appears to have knocked over/broken the mirror and a vase — the physical comedy of someone who can't hold it together even while declaring their intentions
jokes.explanation: Inferred physical comedy — the requests to clean up the mirror and vase mid-monologue imply Tobias broke them. Functional physical comedy that undercuts his earnest speech without being exceptional.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I'll show them a little T and A.' — George Michael's reaction shot
jokes.setup: Tobias is planning to coach Ann in the pageant
jokes.punchline: Tobias uses the phrase 'T and A' (which Maeby used to mean sexual appeal) in his own context — presumably meaning Tobias and Ann — oblivious to how it sounds
jokes.explanation: A callback that works because of Tobias's unique gift for saying sexually loaded things innocently. The 'T and A' here likely means 'Tobias and Ann' which is a running Tobias gag of accidentally double entendres. George Michael's reaction grounds it. Classic Tobias character comedy.

jokes.text: Tobias on coaching Ann: 'Within her lies a queen. Let me out that queen.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is explaining his plan to coach Ann for the pageant
jokes.punchline: 'Let me out that queen' — an inadvertent (or fully advertent) double entendre about Tobias's implied closeted homosexuality, with 'queen' doing double duty as pageant winner and gay slang
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's Tobias highlights. 'Queen' in both the pageant sense and the gay slang sense — and the phrase 'let me out that queen' maps directly onto the 'coming out' idiom. Four words, maximum density, maximally character-specific (this joke ONLY works from Tobias). Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Yeah, I think you just did.' — after Tobias's 'let me out that queen' line
jokes.setup: Tobias says 'Let me out that queen'
jokes.punchline: George Michael flatly confirms the double entendre has already occurred — a teenage boy's deadpan observation of an adult's inadvertent self-revelation
jokes.explanation: Six words that acknowledge what just happened without belaboring it. The show's house style at its best — let the joke breathe, one beat of acknowledgment, move on. George Michael's deadpan is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Come on, I lie to yours all the time.' — responding to Michael refusing to lie to Steve Holt
jokes.setup: Michael says 'I'm not gonna lie to your son'
jokes.punchline: Gob's casual confession that he routinely lies to George Michael — offered as a reasonable quid pro quo
jokes.explanation: Gob as a terrible adult. The shamelessness of the admission — not as a confession but as a negotiating point — is the joke. 'All the time' is the key word: not occasionally, not by accident, but habitually and routinely. Very Gob.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Third place, although a little bit plain, has super low self-esteem. So I step in and, uh, lay her crown upon my sweet head.'
jokes.setup: Gob's taxonomy of pageant contestants continues to third place
jokes.punchline: 'Lay her crown upon my sweet head' is a spectacularly purple euphemism for sex that also technically describes a literal crowning ceremony — Gob's romantic self-image is as absurd as his behavior
jokes.explanation: The metaphor is absurd and grandiose. 'My sweet head' is the detail that makes it — it's both the most self-aggrandizing and the most ridiculous possible phrasing. The follow-up 'Have I missed this euphemism?' from Michael confirms the joke landed.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Have I missed this euphemism?'
jokes.setup: Gob's 'lay her crown upon my sweet head' monologue
jokes.punchline: Michael's flat inquiry whether he's correctly parsed the sexual euphemism — more concerned with linguistic accuracy than the ethical content of Gob's confession
jokes.explanation: Michael's response is perfectly calibrated — not outrage, not amusement, just a sincere question about semantics. The joke is that he's prioritizing comprehension over the actual content. One of Michael's funniest modes: earnest in the face of absurdity.

jokes.text: Steve Holt! — Steve Holt announces himself by name with a fist pump
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to avoid his son
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt arrives yelling his own name — his recurring character tic of enthusiastically self-identifying
jokes.explanation: The 'Steve Holt!' running gag is one of the show's most beloved — a young man so enthusiastically self-promoting that he announces himself by name with a fist pump. High quotability, high character integration. Simple but executed with perfect commitment.

jokes.text: Michael: 'He's hiding from me, isn't he?' — Steve Holt's accurate read of the situation
jokes.setup: Gob has just fled at Steve's arrival
jokes.punchline: Steve correctly identifies that he's being avoided but asks with a resigned gentleness rather than anger — revealing he expected this
jokes.explanation: Steve Holt's self-awareness about being abandoned by Gob adds emotional texture to what could be a pure joke. The comedy is in his resigned acceptance — he's been through this before. Effective character work.

jokes.text: Michael: 'You know, you got to stop saying your name all the time.'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt has just yelled 'Steve Holt!' on arrival
jokes.punchline: Michael addresses the running gag directly — a meta-acknowledgment of Steve's defining character quirk
jokes.explanation: The show acknowledging its own running gag through Michael. Not quite meta enough to be fully self-referential, but it rewards viewers who've noticed the pattern. The delivery matters here — Michael is genuinely trying to give friendly advice.

jokes.text: Steve Holt! / Uncle Mike! (repeated) Both men felt good about themselves.
jokes.setup: Michael invites Steve Holt to do the triathlon; Steve is elated
jokes.punchline: Both men enthusiastically congratulate each other ('Uncle Mike!' / 'Steve Holt!') in a self-congratulatory loop — the narrator's dry observation 'both men felt good about themselves' undercuts the display
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan 'both men felt good about themselves' is the key joke here — it defuses the sincere male-bonding moment with gentle mockery. The circularity of their celebration (each shouting the other's preferred title) is the visual/audio gag.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I'll just have to steal something of his. Like, say, his bicycle. As a... placeholder.'
jokes.setup: Gob is jealous that Michael has bonded with Steve Holt
jokes.punchline: Gob's revenge plan is to steal Michael's bicycle — not as a vehicle, not to deprive him of transport, but as a symbolic 'placeholder' with no clear logic. The word 'placeholder' makes it inexplicably worse.
jokes.explanation: The joke is 'placeholder' — a corporate/technical word deployed by a man who barely understands human relationships. It suggests Gob has thought about this just enough to use the word without understanding what he means by it. The bicycle is also funny: not a car, not his wallet, a bicycle. Gob at his most childishly illogical.

jokes.text: Michael's voicemail to Rita announcing the triathlon — 'Just want to let you know that I'm running the triathlon at the fair, and if you're back in t...' — immediately embarrassed by himself mid-message
jokes.setup: Michael said he wasn't being clingy; he's now leaving a voicemail about an athletic event clearly intended to impress Rita
jokes.punchline: He trails off mid-message, catching himself being exactly as desperate as he said he wasn't
jokes.explanation: A nice beat of self-awareness that continues Michael's clinginess irony arc. Not as sharp as the earlier versions of this joke but functions well as a character-continuity note.

jokes.text: Maeby's pageant disguise: the Nicole Kidman prosthetic nose from The Hours, purchased by Jamie Kennedy at a charity auction for $10,000
jokes.setup: Maeby is entering the pageant in disguise as 'Surely Woolfbeak'
jokes.punchline: Her disguise is a specific, real prosthetic nose from a specific film, with a specific buyer (Jamie Kennedy) and specific price — the mundane administrative detail of how she obtained it undercuts the absurdity perfectly
jokes.explanation: The specificity is everything — not 'a fake nose' but Nicole Kidman's nose from The Hours, purchased by Jamie Kennedy at charity. The detail 'by Jamie Kennedy' is an odd, specific non-sequitur that rewards viewers who know Kennedy is a prankster comedian. The $10,000 price elevates the absurdity. Maeby as a child studio executive having attended a charity auction is its own background gag.

jokes.text: It's Surely. I'm just here to prove that all these pageants care about is looks. Just watch, Surely's inner beauty will get trumped by her outer icky.
jokes.setup: Maeby has entered the pageant in disguise as 'Surely Woolfbeak' — a fake disabled contestant with a prosthetic nose
jokes.punchline: 'Outer icky' is Maeby's invented phrase for her disguise's unattractiveness — she plans to prove pageants are superficial by losing while in costume
jokes.explanation: 'Outer icky' is a nicely awkward invented phrase. The plan itself (deliberately losing a beauty pageant to make a point) is an entertaining absurdist premise.

jokes.text: Jamie Kennedy: 'Oh, you're such a dear. But I'm not putting you in my remake of A Thoroughly Polite Dustup.'
jokes.setup: Jamie Kennedy purchased the nose at a charity auction
jokes.punchline: The title 'A Thoroughly Polite Dustup' is a joke in itself — an absurdly mild descriptor for what sounds like a boxing film, and Kennedy refusing to cast Maeby (a child) is its own absurdist non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: The film title is the joke — 'A Thoroughly Polite Dustup' as a name for a movie Maeby apparently asked to be in during a charity auction interaction. The scene is brief but absurdist in a characteristically AD way. Slight because it's a quick aside.

jokes.text: Pageant official: 'Miss Woolfbeak, although it costs quite a bit to put in a ramp for you, we got approval. You're in.' — accepting Maeby's disguise because she appears disabled
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to enter the inner beauty pageant with a prosthetic nose disguise
jokes.punchline: The pageant accepts her not because of the nose but because they interpret her as being in a wheelchair — accommodating her 'disability' at great expense, for a disguise
jokes.explanation: The irony is that the pageant's inclusive gesture (wheelchair accessibility) enables Maeby's scheme to disprove their inclusivity. The dark subtext — an 'inner beauty' pageant spending money on a ramp while presumably not actually caring about inner beauty — is layered and smart.

jokes.text: Lucille on Buster losing a hand: 'My God, he's already lost a hand. If he loses a leg, how will we ever get him out of the apartment?'
jokes.setup: Lucille is meeting with attorney Bob Loblaw about getting Buster out of the Army
jokes.punchline: Her concern about Buster's injury is entirely selfish — she worries not about his safety but about her practical problem of getting him out of her apartment
jokes.explanation: Lucille as the world's most selfish mother, using concern about her son's combat injuries to express her problem — getting him out of HER apartment. 'How will we ever get him out of the apartment' is the perfect monstrous pivot. High character integration — only Lucille Bluth could say this.

jokes.text: Buster's hook getting stuck in the prize hole: 'Oh, it got stuck in the prize hole again.' — 'I told you not to play that stupid game anymore.' — 'I wasn't playing it. I was just trying to get my other one back.'
jokes.setup: Buster has a hook for a hand and apparently has a history with a claw-machine prize hole
jokes.punchline: Not only has this happened before ('again'), but Buster was trying to retrieve a PREVIOUS hook — implying this has happened at least twice, and each time he loses the retrieval hook as well
jokes.explanation: The escalation is what makes this exceptional: it's not just that the hook is stuck (bad enough), it's that it's happened before ('again'), AND Buster clarifies he was trying to retrieve a PREVIOUS hook (implying a recursive disaster). Each revelation is funnier than the last. 'There goes number three' from the narrator would be even better. Classic Arrested Development escalating absurdity with Buster.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'I'll do anything to get out of this (bleep) apartment.' Lindsay (misheard): 'You'll do anything to get out of (bleep)-ing in this apartment.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. complains about being confined to the apartment
jokes.punchline: Lindsay mishears 'get out of this apartment' as 'get out of (bleep)-ing in this apartment' — transforming a complaint about confinement into a complaint about sex location. The bleep lands double duty again.
jokes.explanation: Another use of the bleep-as-wordplay mechanic, this time as mishearing rather than cultural translation. The episode is building a whole vocabulary of bleep comedy. Lindsay's misinterpretation says as much about her preoccupations (sex) as George Sr.'s words do about his (confinement). The show is having fun with the FCC.

jokes.text: 'That spells Army' — Buster discovering that 'At Risk Male Youth' is an acronym for ARMY
jokes.setup: Buster is told about a 'Startled Straight' program for 'At Risk Male Youth'
jokes.punchline: Buster realizes the acronym spells 'ARMY' — as if the government has been fishing for recruits through this program all along
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever wordplay setup — 'At Risk Male Youth' is a real-sounding program name until you clock the acronym, and the narrator's 'the government had been fishing in this pool for years' contextualizes it as a dark conspiracy joke. Original, well-structured, earned. One of the episode's sharper constructions.

jokes.text: George Sr. on triathlon difficulty: 'People lose control of every bodily function. Didn't you ever see that video? It's like your mother on New Year's Eve. Actually, Lucille went down faster.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. warns Michael that triathlons are physically brutal — citing a famous video of athletes losing control
jokes.punchline: 'It's like your mother on New Year's Eve' — Lucille has publicly lost bodily control at a party. Then 'Actually, Lucille went down faster' — she was worse than elite athletes
jokes.explanation: George Sr. comparing the triathlon's notorious physical toll to Lucille's New Year's performance is inspired, but the real punch is 'Lucille went down faster' — implying she was worse than the worst case of athletic failure. The escalation ('actually') is well-timed.

jokes.text: George Sr. accidentally functions as the 'Startled Straight' example by scaring Michael with triathlon horror stories — 'You see? Perfect for this Startled Straight thing.'
jokes.setup: Michael says George Sr. isn't right for the Startled Straight program
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s rant accidentally scares Michael — proving the concept, winning himself the gig by accident
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/payoff — George Sr.'s accidental demonstration of exactly the behavior Michael said he couldn't do. The irony is neat and efficient.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I think we need something with a little more spectacle in the act. Something that will make you look small and pretty in comparison. A camel.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is coaching Ann for the talent portion
jokes.punchline: The 'something to make you look small and pretty' sets up a logical (if backhanded) standard — then 'A camel' is the most absurd possible solution, delivered with complete confidence
jokes.explanation: The logic is internally consistent (a camel IS large) which makes the absurdity land harder. Tobias's complete confidence in this solution — no hedging, just 'A camel' — is the key. The show will pay this off with the actual camel act later, which is a great structural choice.

jokes.text: Ann: 'I'll sing to the camel.' Tobias: 'Yes, we can Google some disco songs with the word hump in them.' Ann: 'No, that sounds a bit racy.'
jokes.setup: Tobias proposes using a camel for Ann's act
jokes.punchline: 'Hump' as disco song content — Tobias thinking of 'The Humps' while ostensibly planning a wholesome act. Ann's objection that it's 'racy' is ironic since she presumably doesn't get the double meaning.
jokes.explanation: The word 'hump' doing double duty (camel anatomy / sexual slang) is textbook AD. Ann's objection ('racy') suggests she catches the implication while Tobias may not — or vice versa. Decent layered wordplay.

jokes.text: Tobias to George Michael: 'He may be afraid of sex, but you're not going to win without it.' George Michael: '...' Tobias: 'Oh, good. Have sex with this girl right now. Do it, go.'
jokes.setup: Tobias tells George Michael he 'may be afraid of sex' (referring to someone else — possibly himself, possibly the pageant context)
jokes.punchline: Tobias immediately tests this by commanding George Michael to have sex with Ann on the spot — with parental urgency and zero appropriateness
jokes.explanation: Tobias's complete comfort ordering a teenager to 'have sex with this girl right now' as if it's a drill is deeply wrong and therefore very funny. The 'I didn't think so' when George Michael doesn't comply is Tobias treating this as a failed test rather than a wildly inappropriate command. Great character-specific cringe.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'Ann, you need to decide whether you want a man or a boy. I know how I'd answer.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is telling Ann she needs a real man to coach her
jokes.punchline: 'I know how I'd answer' — Tobias is saying he prefers men, inadvertently confirming his implied homosexuality while ostensibly talking about Ann's romantic choices
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest Tobias double entendres. 'I know how I'd answer' is maximally economical — six words that work perfectly in the surface reading (Tobias endorsing manhood) and the subtext (Tobias revealing his preference for men). The character integration is exceptional: ONLY Tobias can deliver this joke. High quotability.

jokes.text: Training montage: 'Don't ask can I? Ask I can!' / 'You can control your bladder when you're dead.' / 'No blood, no oil!' / 'There's no I in win.'
jokes.setup: Michael is training for the triathlon with a motivational coach
jokes.punchline: The motivational slogans are deliberately mangled: 'There's no I in win' (there is), 'You can control your bladder when you're dead,' and 'No blood, no oil' as a political mashup of athletic and protest slogans
jokes.explanation: 'There's no I in win' is the centerpiece — a deliberate corruption of the real phrase ('no I in team') that is both wrong and not even close to correct. 'You can control your bladder when you're dead' is dark-absurdist and will pay off with the triathlon's incontinence theme. 'No blood, no oil' mashes up athletic and political slogans strangely. Fun sequence.

jokes.text: Gob yelling 'Does anyone know why my handlebars are in my bed?!'
jokes.setup: Gob announced he would steal Michael's bicycle as a 'placeholder' of revenge
jokes.punchline: Gob now has a bicycle in his bed and has apparently forgotten his own plan — or can't figure out what to do with his own stolen property
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback execution. The gap between Gob's stated plan ('I'll steal his bicycle') and his confused relationship with his own stolen property ('why are my handlebars in my bed?!') is maximally funny. The specificity of 'handlebars' — not 'bicycle' — suggests the bike is somehow disassembled in his bed, making it even funnier. One of the episode's best moments.

jokes.text: Announcer: 'And now, please welcome Annabelle Veal, performing We Three Kings with a camel tow.'
jokes.setup: Tobias proposed the camel act earlier
jokes.punchline: The camel act has actually been assembled — Ann performing a Christmas carol ('We Three Kings') with a camel in an inner beauty pageant at a Church and State Fair. 'Camel tow' is also a possible pun (camel toe).
jokes.explanation: The callback to Tobias's absurd camel proposal pays off magnificently — not only did they get a camel, they've woven in a religious song (perfect for the Church and State Fair), and 'camel tow' may be a veiled 'camel toe' pun. The combination of all these elements is peak AD absurdism.

jokes.text: Steve Holt gives Michael pills he calls 'Oxy-Incontinent' — 'they keep you from being incontinent.' Narrator: 'Steve didn't have that quite right.'
jokes.setup: Michael is sore from training; Steve has pills from a coach to help with the triathlon's incontinence problem
jokes.punchline: 'Oxy-Incontinent' is Steve's misunderstanding of 'OxyContin' — the narrator confirms Steve has the drug name wrong, implying Michael just took an opioid
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark comedy construction: Steve's cheerful malapropism ('Oxy-Incontinent') perfectly disguises an opioid drug reference that the narrator then gently confirms. 'Steve didn't have that quite right' is one of the best narrator understatements of the episode — Michael has unknowingly taken OxyContin. The incontinence theme from earlier (George Sr.'s warning, the training montage) pays off here. Will also pay off later when Michael feels terrible. Excellent layered structure.

jokes.text: Maeby (as Surely Woolfbeak) at the pageant Q&A: 'How has God influenced your life?' — 'About just as much as Big Bird and the Keebler elves.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is trying to get eliminated from the pageant; a Pope impersonator asks about God's influence
jokes.punchline: She equates God with Big Bird and the Keebler Elves — all equally fictional entities in her view — delivered to a church-sponsored pageant
jokes.explanation: Maeby's atheism delivered bluntly to a Pope impersonator at a church pageant is exactly right for her character. 'Big Bird and the Keebler elves' is a specific, funny trio — not just 'Santa Claus' but chosen to be maximally dismissive while remaining technically innocent. Sharp and character-specific.

jokes.text: Well, my faith would've been shaken if He had taken my legs, too.
jokes.setup: The pageant contestant with a disability offers her faith answer immediately after Maeby's atheist bomb
jokes.punchline: Her answer about God not taking her legs (implying faith is maintained because He only took so much) is both darkly funny and inadvertently competitive — and it devastates Maeby's plan to sabotage her own chances
jokes.explanation: The contrast with Maeby's answer is perfect — the real contestant inadvertently wins the audience by being both devout and resilient. The dark humor is that she's grateful God only took her legs and not more. Works as a cringe contrast with Maeby's sabotage attempt.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Definitely not my shallow, materialistic mother, who only cares about looks.' Judge: 'I think we all feel that way about our mothers, and we all have legs.'
jokes.setup: Gob explains which family member is NOT his favorite, pivoting to insult Lucille
jokes.punchline: The judge's 'I think we all feel that way about our mothers' is an unexpected moment of solidarity — but 'and we all have legs' reminds us all that Maeby/Surely is in a wheelchair, making the judge's generic empathy accidentally cruel
jokes.explanation: The judge's 'we all have legs' addendum is perfectly dark — an accidentally insensitive statement that would be cruel if Surely were actually disabled, but is just absurd since she isn't. The callback to the wheelchair disguise pays off here in an unexpected way. Strong structural comedy.

jokes.text: George Sr. wanders into the wrong 'Startled Straight' tent — the church-sponsored one designed to scare gay men into heterosexuality
jokes.setup: George Sr. is supposed to speak at a program to scare at-risk youth away from crime
jokes.punchline: He's in the wrong tent — this one scares gay men into heterosexuality, a completely different kind of 'straight'
jokes.explanation: The 'Church and State Fair' setting enables this joke — the church-sponsored 'Startled Straight' has a completely different agenda than the secular one. The wordplay on 'straight' (criminal vs. heterosexual) is the joke's engine. Well-constructed and earns its dark subversiveness.

jokes.text: George Sr. in the wrong tent, asking the audience: 'Do you want to become some guy's girlfriend? You want to have some guy reach around you in the middle of the night, start messing with your junk?' Gay man in audience: 'Is he ugly?' — 'No, it's pitch black. You don't see him.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. thinks he's scaring at-risk youth away from prison; he's actually describing prison sexual assault to a gay conversion audience
jokes.punchline: The audience member's 'Is he ugly?' treats George Sr.'s horror story as a potentially appealing scenario — and George Sr. responds with logistical details as if taking the question seriously
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s complete failure to realize his audience has inverted the intended reaction is the joke. The 'Is he ugly?' question is a perfect subversion of his intended horror, and his literal response ('No, it's pitch black') shows him engaging with the question on its own terms without clocking what's happening. Dark but well-executed.

jokes.text: George Sr.: 'It never stops, guys.' Audience Member: 'Is there a cover charge?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is trying to make prison sound horrible; the audience keeps responding as if he's advertising an opportunity
jokes.punchline: 'Is there a cover charge?' — treating the scenario as a nightclub rather than a deterrent
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the tent confusion — each audience response ups the ante. 'Is there a cover charge?' applies nightclub logic to prison rape, which is so wrong it's genuinely funny. Short, sharp, high economy.

jokes.text: Maeby reveals she can walk — standing up to give her crown to Ann — and the pageant audience mistakes it for a miracle: 'She can stand! The Lord's blessed her!'
jokes.setup: Maeby's wheelchair disguise has convinced everyone she's disabled
jokes.punchline: When she stands up to give her crown away, the crowd interprets it as a divine miracle at the Church and State Fair
jokes.explanation: The Church and State Fair setting pays off perfectly here — of course a church-adjacent crowd would interpret a woman standing up from a wheelchair as a miracle. The callback to both the wheelchair disguise and the religious setting is precisely engineered. 'The Lord's blessed her!' is a great line. This is a well-constructed comedic climax for the Maeby plotline.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals the triathlon is tricycles, inflatable pools, and fathers/sons manacled together. Michael: 'What the hell is this?'
jokes.setup: Michael has been rigorously training for a grueling triathlon
jokes.punchline: The 'triathlon' is a children's obstacle course with tricycles and inflatable pools; the 'manacled together' element is both absurd and logistically explains all subsequent physical comedy
jokes.explanation: The long build (training montage, OxyContin pills, physical soreness) pays off against an absurdly modest triathlon. The reveal of tricycles undermines all of Michael's suffering beautifully. The manacling is both a physical comedy setup AND later enables the Gob key subplot. Well-structured long-form misdirection.

jokes.text: Steve Holt: 'They change it every year, so you can't replicate it on your lawn.'
jokes.setup: Michael is furious that Steve knew the triathlon was this children's course
jokes.punchline: Steve's defense includes the detail that the course changes annually — and that you therefore can't practice it on your lawn — suggesting that lawn-replication is a reasonable training metric
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'on your lawn' is the joke — Steve's frame of reference for athletic training is lawn-based home practice. Delivered earnestly, it reveals Steve's own oddly domestic understanding of sport preparation. Nicely character-specific.

jokes.text: Steve Holt: 'I just thought you wanted to hang out and do guy stuff.'
jokes.setup: Michael confronts Steve for letting him over-train for a children's triathlon
jokes.punchline: Steve's entirely sincere reason — he wasn't thinking about the race, he just wanted to bond — is both sweet and a perfect deflation of Michael's outrage
jokes.explanation: The emotional sincerity undercuts Michael's anger and reframes Steve as genuinely just wanting connection — which makes the whole training comedy sequence retroactively bittersweet. A nice tonal note.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I was worried that if you came in first you'd outgrown me.' Ann: 'Outgrown you. That is the term I've been looking for.'
jokes.setup: George Michael shares his insecurity; he was afraid Ann would outgrow him if she won
jokes.punchline: Ann seizes on 'outgrown you' as the perfect description of how she already feels — she's been looking for this word, confirming she has indeed outgrown him
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony is devastating and funny simultaneously: George Michael inadvertently gives Ann the vocabulary she needed to break up with him. 'That is the term I've been looking for' is a genuinely great line — it implies Ann has been circling this feeling without words and George Michael helpfully provided them. Dark, well-structured, high economy.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It seems Gob was right about what happens to third-place contestants.' — Ann has met a man (Tobias)
jokes.setup: Gob predicted third-place contestants have low self-esteem and are susceptible to being 'crowned'
jokes.punchline: Ann has immediately found a man — and based on context, it's Tobias, making the 'man' in question doubly ironic
jokes.explanation: The callback to Gob's pageant taxonomy pays off with Ann — a third-place contestant — immediately finding a 'man' (presumably Tobias, which creates its own layer of comedy given Tobias's characterization). The narrator's dry confirmation of Gob's thesis is well-placed.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I don't know what was in that pill, but I do not feel great.' Steve: 'Gonna get a little worse before it gets better.'
jokes.setup: Michael took 'Oxy-Incontinent' (OxyContin) before the race
jokes.punchline: His body is now reacting; Steve's cheerful 'gonna get a little worse' suggests he knows what the pill actually was
jokes.explanation: Steve's 'gonna get a little worse before it gets better' is the kicker — he seems to know what the pill was, confirming the dark reading. The OxyContin thread pays off through Michael's deteriorating physical state, which then enables the comedy of him trying to stop George Michael from enlisting while increasingly impaired.

jokes.text: Hey, do this with me! / And that's when Rita noticed Michael. / Wait for me! Wait for me!
jokes.setup: Michael has just finished the triathlon, is high on Oxy-Incontinent, and discovers George Michael is signing up for the Army
jokes.punchline: Michael, still chained to Steve Holt and apparently impaired, tries to run after George Michael — dragging Steve with him — while screaming 'wait for me!'
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy beat — Michael dragging Steve while impaired works as escalation but isn't particularly inventive on its own. The comedy is more situational than crafted.

jokes.text: George Sr. in a Humvee at the fair, attempting to escape — then choosing between the 'bulletproof' Pope Mobile and the Army Combat Humvee, 'and he decided to escape in the bulletproof one.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. wants to escape; two vehicles are available — a Humvee and a Pope Mobile
jokes.punchline: He picks the Pope Mobile believing it to be bulletproof (because Popes use them). The 'On the next...' confirms it is not: 'a fake Pope Mobile can't stop real bullets.'
jokes.explanation: The setup of a 'fake' Pope Mobile at the Church and State Fair paying off with real bullets in the next-episode teaser is classically AD — the season-arc joke lands as a one-episode setup. George Sr.'s logic (real Popes use bulletproof vehicles, therefore this replica must be) is internally consistent idiocy.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'There's all sorts of benefits, and it's like a family — but it's also an army of one.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is defending his decision to join the Army
jokes.punchline: 'A family — but it's also an army of one' — misremembering the Army's 'Army of One' slogan and inadvertently juxtaposing it with 'family' to create a contradiction
jokes.explanation: A gentle joke on Army recruitment slogans — a 'family' and an 'army of one' are contradictions that George Michael doesn't notice. Mild but fits his earnest naivety.

jokes.text: And it's no place for wimps. George Michael, you have a family, okay, and I'm the wimp, not you... because I could not take you being away in the Army.
jokes.setup: George Michael is about to enlist; Michael tries to stop him
jokes.punchline: Michael's heartfelt admission — 'I'm the wimp, not you' — is both sincere AND an inadvertent callback to the episode's central 'pussycat' theme (being called a wimp/pussy all episode)
jokes.explanation: More emotional than comedic, but the callback to the episode's masculinity theme landing here is intentional. The comedy is gentle and earned rather than laugh-out-loud.

jokes.text: Michael to George Michael: 'I'm the wimp, not you... because I could not take you being away in the Army.'
jokes.setup: George Michael joined because he thought being in the Army proved manliness
jokes.punchline: Michael resolves the manliness theme by redefining it: admitting he's the 'wimp' because he can't handle George Michael leaving — a sweet, comedically-delivered emotional admission
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic resolution — 'wimp' gets redefined through emotional honesty. Sweet more than funny, but the self-deprecating framing gets a gentle laugh. Functional emotional payoff.

jokes.text: Gob: 'That's too bad. I was this close to getting out.'
jokes.setup: George Michael deciding not to join the Army foils Gob's plan to use him to get Buster out
jokes.punchline: Gob's response to a tender father-son moment is pure selfishness — his disappointment is entirely about his own exit strategy, not about George Michael
jokes.explanation: Gob's inability to register the emotional weight of the preceding scene — delivering a selfish complaint immediately after a touching moment — is characteristically Gob and lands well as a comedic beat.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I think I'm gonna go get one of those corn dog crosses.' Buster: 'With all the crucifixins?'
jokes.setup: They're at the Church and State Fair wrapping up the plot
jokes.punchline: 'Crucifixins' — 'fixins' for a corn dog rendered as 'crucifixins' at a church fair. A portmanteau that perfectly summarizes the whole premise.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best wordplay moments. 'Crucifixins' is a genuinely delightful portmanteau — 'fixins' (corn dog condiments) + 'crucifixion' — that only works at a Church and State Fair. It's the kind of word that gets quoted. High originality: you couldn't have predicted this word. Michael's casual setup ('corn dog crosses') is also a good pun. Together they're a mini comedy sequence. High quotability.

jokes.text: Rita: 'You're a complete and utter (bleep).' Narrator: 'I can't believe we had to bleep that. It would have been such a nice moment.'
jokes.setup: Rita calls Michael a '(bleep)' — the episode's central wordplay joke — in a sincerely romantic moment
jokes.punchline: The narrator breaks the fourth wall to lament that the bleep ruins what should have been a sweet scene — the meta-commentary on the FCC/censorship mechanism that powers all the episode's bleep jokes
jokes.explanation: The episode's comedic climax — bringing back the central bleep joke at the most emotionally resonant moment, then having the narrator explicitly mourn the censorship that the episode has been exploiting all along. 'I can't believe we had to bleep that. It would have been such a nice moment.' is the narrator stepping out of the show to acknowledge the show's own joke architecture. This is meta-comedy executed at a high level: the censorship mechanism that was used for wordplay all episode is now lamented as preventing genuine emotion. Near-perfect structural comedy.

jokes.text: 'On the next Arrested Development': Buster signs Steve Holt up for the Army — 'I'm finally ready to be a father to you. I know that now.' Steve: 'I don't go in for another two years.' Gob: 'Well, as your father, can't I sign something?'
jokes.setup: Gob rejected fatherhood all episode; Buster is bumbling
jokes.punchline: Buster has already signed Steve up for the Army — making him a worse father than Gob who at least only ran away. Then Gob swoops in claiming fatherhood to undo the Army enrollment — but his method ('can't I sign something?') suggests he'll sign Steve up for something worse.
jokes.explanation: The 'On the next...' segment delivers a legitimately funny callback — Buster doing to Steve what he himself was trying to avoid, and Gob's sudden paternal embrace ('as your father, can't I sign something?') being immediately suspicious. The show's 'On the next...' segments are often jokes themselves, and this one pays off the episode's themes efficiently.

jokes.text: 'On the next': George Sr. escaping in a fake Pope Mobile — 'a fake Pope Mobile can't stop real bullets.' George Sr.: 'Thank God I wasn't in the Humvee.'
jokes.setup: George Sr. escaped in what he thought was the bulletproof vehicle
jokes.punchline: He thanks God he wasn't in the Humvee — the actual bulletproof Army vehicle — while currently in a fake Pope Mobile being shot at
jokes.explanation: George Sr. thanking God he avoided the SAFER vehicle while currently being shot in the UNSAFE vehicle is perfectly constructed irony. 'Thank God I wasn't in the Humvee' is a great final line for the episode — it could be a last line for George Sr. entirely. The dark absurdism of the show's 'On the next...' segments being genuinely dangerous to characters elevates this.

tmdb_overview: To prove he's a man to Rita, Michael and his nephew Steve Holt compete in the Church And State Fair's annual triathlon. George Sr. tries to scare some at-risk youth, and Tobias tries to help George-Michael's girlfriend Ann to compete in the fairs' "inner beauty" pageant.
insight: Season 3's densest episode: 72 jokes in 27 minutes, anchored on a single sight-gag premise.
